event_blocks.summary: The video commences with upbeat background music and an on-screen text "Welcome!" over a close-up of hands, wearing a gold bracelet and ring, assembling wooden blocks on a white marble surface. JC, from "Crafting with JC," introduces the video, promising "20 of my favorite Dollar Tree DIYs." The scene shifts to a Dollar Tree store aisle, showcasing craft supplies, before focusing on a completed DIY wooden tealight holder with a glass top. JC explains her intention to create a rustic wooden holder for glass tealight candle holders purchased from Dollar Tree. She selects clear, round glass candle holders and places them on the white marble surface. The sound of blocks rattling is heard as she unpacks a "Tumbling Tower Game" box from Dollar Tree, revealing numerous small wooden blocks. JC states she will use "Dollar Tree's Tumbling Tower blocks," noting a pack contains 72, and plans to use approximately 58 for this project. The hands methodically sort and measure the blocks with a black ruler on the white marble surface, accompanied by the sound of blocks being poured. The crafting process continues on the white marble surface as hands assemble the wooden blocks, creating "four sets" of square frames by gluing four blocks together, using a black ruler for alignment. "Wood glue" from a Dollar Tree bottle is applied, with excess wiped away. JC then gathers 21 blocks, detailing a pattern of gluing one block on its side, followed by four blocks flat, side-by-side, repeating this for the remaining blocks to form the "sides of the wooden holder," and then constructs another matching set with 21 more blocks.

Dollar Tree Store Aisle.description: A brightly lit retail environment with shelves stocked with craft supplies, where the creator purchased the candle holders and the 'Tumbling Tower Game' blocks, as mentioned in her narration.

On-Screen Text.description: Includes 'Welcome!', 'DOLLAR TREE', and 'DIY #1 Wooden Tealight Holder', which are visually displayed while the female creator, 'JC,' introduces herself and the video's theme of '20 of my favorite Dollar Tree DIYs' that are budget-friendly and look high-end.

DIY Wooden Tealight Holder.description: A completed craft project featuring a wooden base with a glass top, styled with greenery and candles, which the creator aims to replicate using the Dollar Tree supplies to create a 'rustic wooden holder.'

Wooden Blocks.description: Small, light-colored rectangular blocks being glued together to form square frames and long strips, specifically in sets of four and then in sequences of 21 to create 'the sides of the wooden holder'. A distinct scraping/sliding sound is heard as they are manipulated.

Black Ruler.description: Used horizontally to guide the alignment of Wooden Blocks during assembly, ensuring straight and even structures, as the crafter meticulously follows the narrated steps.

Tumbling Tower Game Box.description: A blue cardboard box labeled 'Tumbling Tower Game' containing wooden blocks, purchased from Dollar Tree. The creator explicitly states she will be using 'Dollar Tree's Tumbling Tower blocks' from this box.

White Marble Surface.description: Serves as the clean, bright workspace for the crafting activity, providing a neutral backdrop for the wooden components, as the crafter explains the process.

Hands.description: Adorned with a gold bracelet and ring, actively assembling Wooden Blocks into frames and strips using glue and a Black Ruler for alignment, while narrating the precise steps and purpose of the crafting process.

Glass Candle Holders.description: Clear, round glass holders selected from a store shelf and placed on the marble surface. The creator identifies them as a 'pack of four tealight candle holders from Dollar Tree' and states her intention to make a 'rustic wooden holder for them.'

Glue Bottle.description: A yellow bottle with a white nozzle, containing 'wood glue' specifically mentioned by the crafter as being 'from Dollar Tree', used to apply adhesive to the Wooden Blocks during assembly.

A female voice guides the process, explaining the fitting of rectangular and square pieces and the application of glue to specific sides. As gentle, uplifting background music starts, the hands switch to applying a dark brown Waverly Antique Wax finish using a foam brush, wiping away excess with a damp cloth to achieve a rustic shade and texture. The distinct sounds of wiping and buffing accompany the application, with the speaker emphasizing thorough wiping for a beautiful, dry finish. The completed wooden structure, now with an aged appearance and enhanced grain, is presented, and four clear Glass Candle Holders are placed into its compartments, completing the DIY Wooden Tealight Holder.

The person speaking (implied to be the owner of the hands) provides detailed instructions and commentary on the crafting process, explaining how to connect "rectangular pieces" and "square pieces," apply glue, and use "Waverly Antique Wax" with a "foam brush" and "damp cloth" to achieve a "rustic shade and texture" that "dries beautifully."

event_blocks.summary: Hands, wearing a gold bracelet and ring, place three white tealight candles into clear glass holders, which are then positioned within a multi-compartment wooden base, as a narrator describes the process. The narrator then introduces four vanilla-scented votive candles from Dollar Tree, explaining they fit the holders but she opts for larger glass holders to avoid the votive candles sticking out. The completed minimalist arrangement is shown from various angles. The scene shifts to a new DIY project, 'DIY #2 Gold Base Vase,' with on-screen text and the narrator introducing a cylinder vase from Dollar Tree. The same hands wrap the vase with beige painter’s tape, pulling and ripping the tape to create a clean edge a few inches from the base, with the narrator explaining the use of masking or painter's tape and the importance of pushing down ripped ends. The workspace is a white marble surface. In a subsequent segment, a clear plastic cup receives a golden liquid identified as metallic gold paint, though visually poured from a Waverly Wax Antique bottle, followed by white baking soda from an orange box and spackle for texture. A hand uses a wooden stick to mix these into a thick golden paste. Using a black foam brush, the hand applies this paste to the lower half of a painter's-taped clear glass vase, brushing downward and ensuring complete dryness before adding a second coat. The tape is removed after the first coat to reveal a crisp gold base. A second coat is applied for full coverage, including the bottom, and a final layer of paint without baking soda is added to prevent chipping and restore vibrancy. The final vase displays a smooth, golden base.

She adds, "You can use masking tape or painter's tape," and then, "Then I apply that on the vase about a few inches from the base with the ripped ends towards the bottom," as the tape is carefully applied around the lower portion of the vase, ensuring a clean edge. The narrator continues, "I want to add a few more rips until I like how it looks. Then I make sure to push down those rip ends because I'm going to be..." as the audio fades, with the bright, clean white marble surface consistently serving as the workspace backdrop.

The segment opens with a close-up of a clear plastic cup on a white marble surface, as a golden liquid, identified by a female speaker as "metallic gold paint" from "FolkArt" (though visually poured from a bottle labeled 'Waverly Wax Antique'), is poured in with a distinct sound. White baking soda from an orange box is then added, along with spackle for texture as the speaker explains, followed by mixing sounds. event_blocks.summary: Hands adorned with a gold bracelet and ring meticulously apply a thick, textured golden paste to a clear glass vase using a foam brush, creating a metallic shine, then carefully peel off painter's tape to reveal a crisp, scalloped edge, resulting in a DIY Gold Base Vase. Transitioning to 'DIY #3 Brass & Wood,' hands arrange brass rings on a white marble surface, placing wooden blocks inside them to form a circular structure aligned with a black ruler. The voice explains using medium-sized brass rings from a pack of two and a 6-inch wood palette. A wall shelf kit, containing a wooden board, white rope, and metal rings, is introduced, with the voice noting prior projects using only the wood. Hands thread the white rope through the board's holes. The voice clarifies using only the ring and rope from the kit, with upbeat music playing. Hands arrange light-colored wooden blocks into a grid on the white marble surface, and the voice mentions needing two popsicle sticks to trim with miter shears. Two large brass rings are prepared for spray painting gold after light sanding. The rings are placed on the surface and connected with a small metal ring, with the voice explaining how the ring opens to slide both large rings inside. A can of metallic gold spray paint is shown. Hands thread white rope, cut to about a foot, through the brass rings and secure it with hot glue from a gray glue gun, applying dots of glue and wrapping the rope precisely. The voice concludes by securing the end with hot glue, creating a textured, golden circular piece with a hanging loop. Later, hands arrange wooden blocks into a rectangular base on a white marble surface, and the voice instructs on applying Waverly antique wax. Large brass rings are placed over the wooden structure, and the voice guides on standing the rings up and fitting them. The assembled wooden structure, referred to as a 'palette,' is flipped and placed on the rings. A yellow glue bottle is used to apply adhesive to secure popsicle sticks to the bottom of the rings for stability. White rope is threaded through the brass rings and wrapped tightly, with a small metal ring used to connect the ends of the brass rings to form a loop for hanging. The rope is wrapped methodically, creating a textured, circular design, with the voice suggesting switching the rope if it's too small, all set to calm instrumental music.

event_blocks.summary: On a white marble surface, hands with a gold bracelet and dark red nail polish meticulously threaded a white rope through a metal ring and wrapped it around a brass ring, securing it with a hot glue gun to create a hanging wooden riser stand, which a calm female voiceover described as charming for displaying decor. The scene then shifted to a new project as blocks rattled, with the voiceover introducing the need for 48 tumbling tower blocks and four square planks. The hands sorted and arranged the light-colored wooden blocks from the Tumbling Tower Game Box, using a black ruler for alignment and a yellow glue bottle to glue the blocks together in a grid pattern, layer by layer, to form a two-piece square wooden riser stand set. The voiceover explained the process of gluing two blocks end-to-end and then forming square frames, emphasizing precision to ensure squares and not rectangles, and noting that asymmetrical sides indicate correct gluing. After completing multiple frames, the hands stacked them, with the voiceover instructing that six squares are needed in total, and four squares for the first riser. The hands then glued and stacked these squares to form a box-like structure, alternating the bottom ends of the blocks as they were stacked to create a pattern, with careful glue application to prevent seepage. The methodical crafting progressed from individual frames to a three-dimensional structure.

The Hands select and arrange the letters 'L', 'O', 'V', and 'E'. The narrator states, "On top of the blocks, I'm going to be gluing these wooden letters that I got from Dollar Tree, and since it is still February, I'm going to spell out love." A small container of gold paint is introduced, and the Hands use a fine-tipped brush to carefully paint each letter gold, transforming them from natural wood to a shiny metallic finish. The narrator adds, "I paint the letters with gold acrylic paint, and this DIY is actually going to be part of DIY number three, as you'll see later. But I thought the piece was perfect on its own as well." The completed gold letters are then placed on the previously painted white blocks, suggesting they will be attached to form a decorative sign, all while the background music continues.

The final arrangement shows the 'LOVE' blocks neatly positioned within the gold tray, completing the decorative piece, which the speaker states uses "the letter blocks that I made from DIY number two" and is "just a continuation of that DIY."

event_blocks.summary: A pair of hands, wearing a black wristband and light blue nail polish, places a light-colored Bamboo Ring onto a Wooden Board situated on a White Marble Surface, with a female narrator explaining the measurement process and emphasizing that the length is customizable as long as it doesn't exceed the board. event_blocks.summary: Hands, wearing transparent plastic gloves and a black wristband, first wrap jute rope around the handles of a light-colored wooden tray, enhancing its stability as a female voice explains the benefit of increased surface area. The finished tray is then presented in a styled setting with candles and greenery, described as an adorable and quick DIY project costing only a few dollars. A title card for 'DIY #10 Wood & Glass Pl...' appears with leafy graphics. The scene shifts to a Dollar Tree Store Aisle where Hands select a 1" x 3" x 2' wooden board from shelves stocked with 'PREMIUM SANDED BOARDS'. The voice discusses purchasing similar boards previously from Home Depot for $6.98 for a pack of four 2-feet long boards, noting a price increase but still affordable at about a dollar a foot, and suggests Dollar Tree offers similar boards for approximately $1.25 a foot. Back in the workspace, the 1" x 3" x 2' wooden board and a 7 and 3/4 inch round glass cutting board from Dollar Tree are laid out. The narrative then focuses on creating a wooden stand for the glass top, with Hands measuring a long wood plank with a black ruler, marking the center for an 8-inch cut. Stained wooden blocks, previously waxed with 'Waverly Wax Antique' and left unstained on the end grain for glue, are arranged. Using Titebond Quick & Thick Multi-Surface Glue, Hands join a 3-inch stained wooden block perpendicularly to the center of an 8-inch piece, then attach another 3.75-inch block to the opposite side, forming a cross. Smaller pieces are glued upright to the four ends, with advice to clamp until dry. The clear glass top is unwrapped, plastic adhesive bumpers are removed, and the glass is wiped clean. Finally, the glass top is placed onto the wooden base and secured with metal fasteners, creating a minimalist wooden riser stand that fits perfectly. The completed riser is styled with a candle and greenery, and the voice expresses satisfaction with the ease of the project.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a female voice exclaiming, "And it looks amazing," over a title card for 'DIY #11 Home Letter Board.' The scene shifts to a close-up of a blue house-shaped wooden sign, reading 'A MERMAID LIVES HERE' in white, positioned on a White Marble Surface. Hands, wearing a gold bracelet and ring, select a yellow 'C's Crafters Square Vinyl Paper Box' containing floral vinyl paper. The speaker notes that "Dollar Tree carries an assortment of vinyl and glitter paper" and she is using "this adorable floral pattern," also mentioning plans to use "wooden farmhouse letter tiles and tumbling tower blocks," accompanied by the sound of blocks clattering. The Hands open the box, revealing dark blue vinyl paper with pink and peach floral patterns, which the speaker states has a sticky back, eliminating the need for glue. The Hands unroll the vinyl, lay it flat, and use a Blue Pen to trace the house-shaped sign's outline, with the speaker confirming, "I place the house on top and then I trace that all around." An on-screen 'SUBSCRIBE' button with a bell icon appears. The Hands then cut the vinyl using scissors, with a subtle cutting sound, and apply it to the sign by peeling back the backing from the bottom and aligning it with the MDF board, pressing down as the backing is removed. Excess vinyl is trimmed with a craft knife, and the edges are sealed and polished with a sanding sponge. Subsequently, the Hands use a Foam Brush to apply a dark brown wood finish, identified as Minwax Dark Walnut, to several light-colored wooden blocks to achieve a warm, antique brown hue, matching the wood strips on the roof. These stained blocks, referred to as "tumbling blocks," are then assembled into a cross-shaped base using Titebond Quick & Thick Multi-Surface Glue, with the speaker explaining the gluing process for three blocks end-to-end for the bottom and two for the middle. The entire crafting process occurs on the White Marble Surface, with light, upbeat instrumental background music playing.

The scene transitions to a close-up of a blue house-shaped wooden sign with 'A MERMAID LIVES HERE' in white, placed on a clean, bright White Marble Surface. The female voice identifies this as an "adorable little MDF house" she's turning into a "customizable letterboard," mentioning an initial step of removing a foam glitter sticker. Hands, adorned with a gold bracelet and a ring, enter the frame and select a yellow 'C's Crafters Square' box containing 'vinyl paper permanent' with a floral design. The speaker explains that "Dollar Tree carries an assortment of vinyl and glitter paper" and she's using "this adorable floral pattern," also noting she'll use "wooden farmhouse letter tiles and tumbling tower blocks," accompanied by the sound of blocks clattering. The Hands open the box, revealing a roll of dark blue vinyl paper with pink and peach floral patterns. The speaker states, "I take out the roll of vinyl, it does have a sticky back so no gluing needed." The Hands unroll the paper, lay it flat, and use a blue pen to trace the outline of the house-shaped sign onto the vinyl, with the speaker confirming, "I place the house on top and then I trace that all around." A 'SUBSCRIBE' button with a bell icon appears in the bottom right corner, encouraging viewer engagement as the precise, deliberate actions of measuring and preparing the vinyl continue.

The video opens with a close-up of a roll of dark blue floral vinyl paper, adorned with pink, peach, and white flowers, being unrolled on a bright white marble surface. A female voice states, "I cut it out," as hands, wearing a gold bracelet and a ring, use scissors to cut a section of the vinyl, accompanied by a subtle cutting sound. The hands then place a house-shaped wooden sign, labeled 'A MERMAID LIVES HERE', onto the vinyl and trace its outline with a blue pen. The speaker explains her application technique: "To apply, I start at the bottom. I like to peel off a little bit of the backing on the bottom and then fold it down," adding, "And I just match the bottom edge of the vinyl to the MDF board and then peel off the backing while pressing down on the vinyl." After cutting the vinyl to match the sign’s shape, the hands carefully apply it, smoothing it down and trimming the excess, with the speaker noting, "I make sure there are no air pockets where the vinyl meets the roof, and then I remove the excess vinyl with a craft knife." She further details, "I seal the edge with a sanding sponge. I sand downwards all around, and that gives the house a nice polished look." Next, the hands use a foam brush to apply a dark brown wood finish to several light-colored wooden blocks, staining them to achieve a warm, antique brown hue. The speaker identifies the product, "I stain the blocks with Minwax Dark Walnut. I do want to keep it dark to match the strips of wood on the roof." The stained blocks, referred to as "tumbling blocks," are then assembled into a cross-shaped base using Titebond Quick & Thick Multi-Surface Glue, with the speaker explaining, "First, I glue three blocks together end to end, and this will go on the bottom of the house. Then I glue two together, and this will go in the middle." Throughout the crafting process, which takes place on the clean, bright white marble surface, a light, upbeat instrumental background music plays.

event_blocks.summary: On a white marble surface, dark-stained wooden blocks and a floral vinyl-covered house-shaped wooden sign are arranged. Gentle, upbeat background music plays as a calm female voice explains the process. Hands, wearing a gold bracelet and ring, use a gray Hot Glue Gun to attach three wooden blocks to the bottom of the sign, creating a frame, with sounds of blocks being placed. The voice then instructs to glue the two middle blocks. Next, the Hands open a clear plastic bag, accompanied by crinkling sounds, revealing wooden Scrabble tiles. The crafter mentions purchasing them from Dollar Tree, noting they came with 26 letters. As the tiles are arranged to spell 'OUR HOME' on the sign, clattering sounds are heard. The completed sign is described as 'absolutely adorable' and displayed on a white surface with scattered Scrabble tiles and a potted green plant. The video transitions to a new DIY project, showcasing stacks of light-colored Wooden Blocks on the white marble surface. The crafter states they will use 16 tumbling tower blocks and five craft cubes, both from Dollar Tree. The Hands begin assembling these blocks into a cross-shaped base, with sounds of blocks being placed. The crafter explains they will glue four blocks together to form a cross and cut down two blocks to about one and five-eighths of an inch for even sides. On-screen text appears, reading 'DIY #12 Wooden Vase'. The Hands continue arranging blocks, using a miter box and saw. Soft, upbeat instrumental music plays as the narrator's calm female voice explains, 'And I just use a miter box and saw to do that.' She continues, 'Once that's cut, I glue them together like so to form a cross,' describing the visual action. The hands apply 'Titebond Original Wood Glue' from a yellow bottle to small, light-colored wooden blocks, carefully aligning and pressing them together, with subtle sounds of the wood pieces clicking into place. The camera zooms in to highlight the precision as the cross-shaped base takes form. The narrator continues, 'Then I apply the glue on the four ends, and I take a block, stand it upright, and glue those together like so,' describing the addition of vertical blocks to create a three-dimensional, star-like structure. She adds, 'And I do that for all sides,' explaining the purpose: 'this is going to be a perfect base for a cylinder vase or even those little bowls that I used in the first project.' The hands continue to add more blocks, with the narrator detailing, 'Then I get a craft cube and glue four tumbling blocks around it,' and later, 'Then I apply glue on the ends and attach a craft cube on each block,' reinforcing the design of what appears to be a DIY wooden riser stand.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins by showcasing two completed DIY Wooden Riser Stands with greenery on a White Marble Surface, followed by a title card for 'DIY #13 Wooden Plant'. Hands, wearing a silver bracelet, place 'Double Craft Sticks' and a 'Tumbling Tower Game Box' on the surface. A female voice explains the need for giant popsicle sticks (10 inches long), 16 Tumbling Tower blocks, and two square wood planks, all sourced from Dollar Tree. The Hands open the Tumbling Tower Game Box, extract Wooden Blocks, break them apart, and arrange them. A brief cut to a Dollar Tree Store Aisle shows craft supplies, with the speaker expressing surprise at finding Tumbling Tower blocks locally. Returning to the White Marble Surface, the Hands use a 'Titebond Original Wood Glue' bottle to assemble the blocks, first gluing four end-to-end, then adding blocks vertically on each end to form a 'U' shape. The speaker then states, "We make another set with the remaining blocks," meticulously assembling a cross-shaped base with Wooden Blocks and glue. She explains the need for nine giant popsicle sticks from Walmart, trimming them to the length of the blocks using a Yellow Pencil for measurement. The trimmed sticks are then glued vertically onto the base, creating a slatted structure, with the bottom stick flushed with the surface. Two more sticks are glued on top, spaced evenly, and the process is repeated for the other glued blocks to form the sides of a planter box. White clamps hold the pieces in place as the speaker explains, 'I stand those sides up and glue a square plank on the ends.' After the glue dries and a quick sanding, the Hands, now wearing Transparent Plastic Gloves, apply 'Dark Walnut from Minwax' Wood Finish using a Foam Brush to the Wooden Box, coating the top, sides, and interior to achieve a rich, rustic tone. The speaker advises dabbing off excess stain and suggests touching up areas with acrylic paint where stain penetration is hindered by glue. Gentle, calm instrumental music accompanies the organized crafting process.

The speaker presents "Option one," suggesting one could "stop right here if you want," noting, "It's so beautiful just like that." She then guides on how to "cut the rope," advising to "find the beginning of the rope" and "cut just around there, at an angle, and then glue it down." The Hands continue the methodical wrapping, ensuring smooth adhesion. Light, instrumental background music plays throughout this process. The speaker introduces "Option number two," stating, "I'm gonna add handles," and instructs to "look for the beginning of the rope" and "extend the rope out to the amount you want, and then continue gluing." The Hands continue wrapping, carefully pressing each coil into place. An inset image appears in the top right corner, showcasing the completed rope-wrapped vase holding artificial greenery, styled on a wooden shelf, providing a glimpse of the final product. The speaker continues, describing how to "do the other side, making sure it's even with the first handle," and then to "cut and glue it down just like the first one," concluding, "you're done with option two." She expresses personal preference, "I like this one even more." Finally, she presents "Option three," declaring, "I'm just going to keep wrapping all the way up," reiterating the importance to "press down firmly to close off any gaps between the ropes" as the Hands continue their precise work, transforming the plain glass vase into a rustic, decorative piece.

raw_segments: The video opens with a top-down view of hands, adorned with a gold watch, meticulously assembling light-colored Wooden Blocks using Titebond Original Wood Glue on a White Marble Surface. A female narrator explains her process, stating she will leave the wood natural, as soft, upbeat background music begins. The hands carefully glue the blocks to form a rectangular frame, which the narrator refers to as 'the plank,' announcing, 'We are almost done. This DIY takes only minutes to complete.' A distinct 'clunk' sound is heard as the frame is placed into a fabric-lined rectangular basket with a black geometric pattern. The narrator clarifies, 'Then I drop in the plank,' and 'I place the glued blocks underneath the organizer, one on each end,' followed by another 'clunk.' The basket is adjusted to fit the wooden frame snugly, and the completed structure is shown holding three small white pots with succulents, creating a decorative planter. The narrator concludes, 'And that is it! A very easy hack to turn this desk organizer into a little planter. I love how it turned out.' The scene transitions to a new DIY project, introduced by on-screen text 'DIY #19 Cork Va' with leaf illustrations, as the background music shifts to a slightly more upbeat, folksy tune. A brown adhesive cork sheet labeled 'Crafter's Square' is shown, which the narrator explains she 'picked up this adhesive cork sheet from Dollar Tree' and thought it 'would look perfect wrapped around the bottom of a cylinder vase.' The hands then cut a piece of white paper with light blue scissors, as the narrator states, 'I'm going to wrap this around two vases, so I cut it in two.' Finally, the hands pour a golden liquid from a dark brown Wax Bottle into a clear plastic cup, preparing a mixture for the next step, with the narrator adding, 'Now you can apply directly on the vase.'

The video opens with a pair of hands, adorned with black and silver bracelets, placing a small clear glass jar on a white paper sheet atop a white marble surface. A female voice explains, "But I'm going to paint them first with the same mixture I use on the first DIY, and that's just paint mixed with baking soda to add some texture." The hands then use a light blue foam brush to apply white paint to the rim of a white cylindrical vase, which is later shown with a brown cork base. The voice continues, "Then I apply with a brush, I paint the bottom, and then I let that dry. I do recommend sealing it to prevent chipping." As the voice adds, "And once everything is dried, I wrap the cork sheet firmly around the base, and then I mark the excess that needs to be trimmed off," the hands trace the vase’s circumference on a brown Crafter's Square adhesive cork sheet using a yellow pencil, then measure and cut the cork with a black ruler and light blue scissors. The voice instructs, "Then with scissors, I cut it off, hoping it will wrap seamlessly around the vase. If not, it's not a big deal, just keep trimming and if you accidentally trim too much, just take that piece that was trimmed off earlier and place that on top of the seam and it will look intentional." The cut cork strip is wrapped around the base of the vase, secured with adhesive, and smoothed into place. The voice further explains, "So to wrap, I peel back a small section to expose the adhesive and then I stick it firmly on the vase and wrap it around." The hands then cut a piece of white paper with the same light blue scissors, fold it, and use it to measure and mark a second cork strip, which is also cut and wrapped around the vase’s base, creating a layered, textured finish. The final product is a white vase with a neatly wrapped cork base, displayed on the clean white marble surface, all set to the backdrop of gentle, uplifting background music.

event_blocks.summary: A hand wearing black and silver bracelets places a finished white cylindrical vase with a cork base onto a white marble surface as upbeat background music plays and a female voice congratulates the viewer on a quick DIY project. The scene shifts to display two completed vases on a wooden shelf with greenery. On-screen text 'DIY #20' appears against a dark background with leaf illustrations, introducing the next project. The speaker announces the use of a clear glass vase from the Dollar Tree, inspired by Pottery Barn brass bowls, and intends to add texture. Hands place the clear glass vase upside down on a white surface next to a patterned plate featuring blue and teal leaf designs. White acrylic paint is applied to the plate, and the speaker explains the addition of baking soda for texture, stating, "I want this to be very textury, so I do add quite a bit of baking soda." The hands, now covered by a blue disposable glove, utilize a foam brush to apply the textured white paint to the exterior of the glass vase, rotating it for uniform coverage. The speaker clarifies that the bottom is not painted to avoid surface contact, but the inside of the bowl is painted due to its large opening. The finished vase exhibits a smooth, textured white finish, and the speaker notes, "So once the first layer has dried..."
